agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby 2025 Annual Municipal Report presented to Council
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to receive and consider the 2025 Annual Municipal Report and a separate Highlights document. This report is a yearly summary of the city's finances, services, and major projects from the past year. It is not a new law or bylaw — it is a presentation of information for Council and the public. The report does not require a vote to approve new rules, but Council may discuss it and ask questions. Business owners should review the report to understand city spending and service changes that could affect their operations.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
7.1
2025 ANNUAL MUNICIPAL REPORT AND ANNUAL MUNICIPAL REPORT HIGHLIGHTS
Purpose: To present the 2025 Annual Municipal Report and 2025 Annual Municipal Report Highlights.

agenda_briefs.title: Burnaby to replace Development Procedures Bylaw, update fees, effective July 1, 2026
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on repealing the current Burnaby Development Procedures Bylaw (2022) and replacing it with a new Development Procedures Bylaw (2026), effective July 1, 2026. The proposal also includes amendments to the Burnaby Building Bylaw and the Burnaby Consolidated Fees and Charges Bylaw. If approved, these changes would update the rules and fees for development applications, building permits, and related processes in Burnaby. This is a proposed decision — it has not been finalized yet.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision on May 12, 2026 — if approved, new application rules and fees take effect July 1, 2026, affecting your development or building project costs and timelines.

key_dates.label: Council vote on new Development Procedures Bylaw 2026
New Development Procedures Bylaw 2026 takes effect

agenda_briefs.source_body:
7.2
PROPOSED REPEAL AND REPLACEMENT OF BURNABY DEVELOPMENT PROCEDURES BYLAW AND RELATED AMENDMENTS TO THE BURNABY BUILDING BYLAW AND BURNABY CONSOLIDATED FEES AND CHARGES BYLAW
Purpose:
To seek Council approval to repeal
Burnaby Development Procedures Bylaw, 2022
and replace it with the proposed
Development Procedures Bylaw, 2026
, to amend the
Burnaby
Building Bylaw, 2016
and
Burnaby
Consolidated Fees and Charges Bylaw.

attachments.title: Proposed Repeal and Replacement of Burnaby Development Procedures Bylaw and Rela

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby to apply for UBCM award on development approvals
agenda_briefs.summary: Council will consider endorsing an application for the 2026 UBCM Community Excellence Awards. This is based on the final report for Phase 3 of the Development Approvals Process (DAP) program. The DAP program aims to make the process of getting development permits faster and simpler. If endorsed, the application would recognize Burnaby's work in improving how it handles development applications. This is a proposed step — no decision has been made yet.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
7.3
DAP PROGRAM - PHASE 3 FINAL REPORT
Purpose: To provide an update on the activities and outcomes of the Development Approvals Process (DAP) program and seek endorsement to apply for the 2026 UBCM Community Excellence Awards.

agenda_briefs.title: Council to vote on contract for mobile devices and data plans
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on a proposed contract award for the supply and delivery of mobile devices, service plans, and data services for city staff. If approved, this contract would set the terms and costs for phones, tablets, and wireless data used by city employees. The decision is on the agenda for an upcoming council meeting and has not yet been finalized.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
7.4
CA - MOBILE DEVICE AND PLANS
Purpose: To obtain Council approval for a Contract Award (CA) for the supply and delivery of mobile devices, services, subscription plans, and other data services.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby Council to vote on aeration and overseeding contract
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on a proposed contract award for aeration and overseeding services. If approved, this would mean the city hires a company to aerate (poke small holes in the soil to let air, water, and nutrients reach the roots) and overseed (spread new grass seed over existing lawns) on city-owned land. This is a routine maintenance contract and does not create new rules or fees for businesses. The decision is up for approval at an upcoming council meeting.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
7.5
CA - AERATION AND OVERSEEDING SERVICES
Purpose:
T
o obtain Council approval for a Contract Award (CA) for aeration and overseeding services.

agenda_briefs.title: Council to vote on contract for Victory St. road upgrades from Patterson to Jubilee
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on awarding a contract for road upgrades on Victory Street, between Patterson Avenue and Jubilee Avenue. If approved, this would fund construction work to improve the road. The decision is on the agenda for an upcoming council meeting and has not yet been finalized.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
7.6
CA - (CB-3233) VICTORY ST. - PATTERSON TO JUBILEE ROAD UPGRADES
Purpose:
To obtain Council approval for a Contract Award (CA) for the Victory St. – Patterson to Jubilee Road Upgrades project.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby council to vote on sidewalk contract for five streets
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on awarding a contract for new sidewalk construction on Triumph, Carleton, Rosser, Gilmore, and Madison streets. This is a proposed decision — if approved, the city would hire a contractor to build the sidewalks. The project is part of Burnaby's ongoing work to improve pedestrian safety and walkability. Business owners on or near these streets should be aware that construction could affect access, parking, or foot traffic if the contract is awarded.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision — if approved, prepare for possible construction disruptions on these five streets.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
7.7
CA - (CA-3299) TRIUMPH, CARLETON, ROSSER, GILMORE, AND MADISON SIDEWALK PACKAGE
Purpose: To obtain Council approval for a Contract Award (CA) for the Triumph, Carleton, Rosser, Gilmore, and Madison Sidewalk Package project.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Contract change for Burnaby's Sidewalk Expansion Program
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on a proposed contract change (a formal amendment to an existing agreement) for the Sidewalk Expansion Program (project EB-8022). If approved, this change would alter the terms of the contract for building new sidewalks in the city. The decision has not been made yet — Council will consider this proposal at an upcoming meeting.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
7.8
CC - (EB-8022) SIDEWALK EXPANSION PROGRAM
Purpose:
To obtain Council approval for a Contract Change (CC) to the (EB-8022) Sidewalk Expansion Program.

agenda_briefs.title: Council to consider comment letters on Metro 2050 land-use amendments for Maple Ridge and Surrey
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to consider sending comment letters to the Metro Vancouver Regional District about two proposed changes to the regional growth strategy, Metro 2050. One change, proposed by the City of Maple Ridge, would allow development on land near North 256 Street. The other, proposed by the City of Surrey, would allow development in the Hazelmere area. These are site-specific amendments that could affect future growth and land use in those communities. Council has not yet decided what to say in the letters — this is a proposal for discussion and a vote at the upcoming meeting.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
7.9
COMMENT LETTERS ON PROPOSED METRO 2050 AMENDMENTS: CITY OF MAPLE RIDGE (NORTH 256 STREET) and SURREY (HAZELMERE)
Purpose:
To inform Council about two different site-specific Metro 2050 Amendments, one proposed by the City of Maple Ridge and the other by the City of Surrey, and to consider forwarding comment letters to the Metro Vancouver Regional District.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby to review street width standards for new developments
agenda_briefs.summary: Council will consider a motion to direct city staff to review Burnaby's street width standards. This is part of the proposed Works and Services Requirements update. If approved, the review could lead to changes in how wide streets must be in new developments. This may affect construction costs, property layouts, and traffic planning for future projects. The decision has not been made yet — council will vote on whether to start the review.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council vote — if approved, the review could change street requirements for your future development projects.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
8.1
COTW - PROPOSED WORKS AND SERVICES REQUIREMENTS
Purpose: To direct staff to review the City's street width standards.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby council to vote on consultant budget cuts for 2026
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposal that would direct staff to prepare a detailed list of consultant budget cuts for 2026, including which departments and functions would be affected. A separate list of consultants that would keep their funding in 2026 would also be created. This is not a final decision yet — council will consider the request at an upcoming meeting. If approved, the lists would help the city decide where to reduce spending on outside consultants next year.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision — if approved, this could affect city contracts your business relies on.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
8.2
FMC - 2026 CONSULTANT FUNDNG AND BUDGET REDUCTIONS
Purpose: To seek Council approval for staff to provide a comprehensive list of consultant budget cuts, including associated functions and departments, and a separate list of consultants retaining funding for 2026.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby Mountain Trail Management Plan update and direction
agenda_briefs.summary: Council will consider a draft update to the Burnaby Mountain Trail Management Plan. This proposed plan could change how trails on Burnaby Mountain are managed, including rules for trail use, maintenance, and access. The decision is not final yet — Council is set to vote on whether to approve the draft plan and give staff direction to move forward. If approved, the plan would guide future trail management and could affect businesses that use or operate near the trails, such as outdoor recreation or tourism operators.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision — if approved, the plan could affect trail access or rules for your business. Contact the City of Burnaby for details.
agenda_briefs.industries: Construction & Development, Hotel & Accommodation, Professional Services
agenda_briefs.source_body:
8.3
PRCC - DRAFT BURNABY MOUNTAIN TRAIL MANAGEMENT PLAN
Purpose: To provide an update and seek Council direction on the Draft Burnaby Mountain Trail Management Plan.

agenda_briefs.title: Council to consider supporting Vancouver Roques Rugby's Bingham Cup 2028 bid
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on a request from Vancouver Roques Rugby to support their bid to host the Bingham Cup in 2028. The Bingham Cup is an international rugby tournament for gay and inclusive teams. Council's support would mean the City backs the bid, which could bring visitors and economic activity to the region. This is a proposed decision — it has not been approved yet.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
8.4
PRCC - VANCOUVER ROQUES RUGBY - BINGHAM CUP 2028 SUPPORT REQUEST
Purpose: To seek Council support for Vancouver Roques Rugby's bid to host the Bingham Cup in 2028.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Non-market housing project at 3961 and 3983 Norland Avenue
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby Council is set to receive an update on a proposed non-market housing project at 3961 and 3983 Norland Avenue. Non-market housing means homes that are rented or sold at below-market rates, often run by a non-profit or government agency, to make them more affordable. This is a proposal only — no final decision has been made yet. Council will consider the update and may vote on next steps at a future meeting.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
9.1
PROPOSED NON-MARKET HOUSING PROJECT AT 3961 AND 3983 NORLAND AVENUE
Purpose: To provide an update on the proposed non-market housing project at 3961 and 3983 Norland Avenue.

agenda_briefs.title: Council to review status update on Burnaby civic projects
agenda_briefs.summary: Council will consider a status update on significant civic projects in Burnaby for April 2026. This report provides an overview of how major city-building projects are progressing. While no specific decisions are being made at this meeting, the update helps council and the public track timelines, budgets, and any delays. Business owners should watch for future council decisions that could affect construction, development, or local infrastructure.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
9.2
FMC - SU: SIGNIFICANT CIVIC PROJECTS - APRIL 2026
Purpose: To provide Council with a status update (SU) on significant civic projects.

agenda_briefs.title: Council to receive status update on major engineering projects
agenda_briefs.summary: Council will consider a status update on major engineering projects for the period of December 2025 to April 2026. This is a report to keep Council informed about the progress of these projects. No decisions or bylaw changes are being proposed at this time — it is an information item only.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
9.3
FMC - SU: MAJOR ENGINEERING PROJECTS - DECEMBER 2025 - APRIL 2026
Purpose: To provide Council with a status update (SU) on major Engineering Projects.

agenda_briefs.title: Council to consider status update on IT initiatives for early 2026
agenda_briefs.summary: Council will receive a status update on the city's IT initiatives for the period of January to March 2026. This is a report to keep council informed about ongoing technology projects and their progress. No decisions or bylaw changes are being proposed at this time — it is an information item only.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
9.4
FMC - SU: IT INITIATIVES - JANUARY TO MARCH 2026
Purpose: To provide Council with a status update (SU) on the IT initiatives for January to March 2026.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby Council to consider Langara College study on inclusion in public recreation
agenda_briefs.summary: Council will receive an update on a research project led by Langara College that looks at how to make public recreation programs and facilities in Burnaby more inclusive, diverse, equitable, and accessible. The study is still underway, and no decisions have been made yet. The findings could lead to future changes in how recreation services are delivered to better serve Burnaby’s diverse communities.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
9.5
PRCC - INCLUSION, DIVERSITY, EQUITY AND ACCESSIBILITY (IDEAS) IN PUBLIC RECREATION RESEARCH IN BURNABY
Purpose: To provide an update on the IDEAs in Public Recreation research project, led by Langara College, outlining its purpose, partnership model, and progress to date, while sharing emerging insights and demonstrating how the work supports improved access, inclusion, and participation in public recreation for Burnaby’s diverse communities.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby Community Safety and Well-Being Plan update for 2026
agenda_briefs.summary: Council will consider a status update on the 2025 Community Safety and Well-Being Plan, which outlines local strategies to improve safety and quality of life. A proposed 2026 update to the plan is also up for discussion. This is not a final decision — Council is set to review the progress report and decide on next steps at an upcoming meeting.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council meeting date and review the proposed 2026 update — it may affect local safety programs and business operations.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
9.6
PSC - SU: BURNABY COMMUNITY SAFETY AND WELL-BEING PLAN PROGRESS REPORT AND 2026 UPDATE
Purpose: To provide Council with a status update (SU) on the 2025 Community Safety and Well-Being Plan.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby Fire Department operations update to February 2026
agenda_briefs.summary: Council will receive a status update on Burnaby Fire Department operations through February 2026. This is a routine report to keep council informed about the fire department's activities, response times, staffing, and equipment status. No new bylaws or fees are being proposed at this time — it is an information-only update for council's awareness.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
9.7
PSC - SU: FIRE DEPARTMENT (FEBRUARY 2026)
Purpose: To provide Council with the Burnaby Fire Department's status update (SU) available to the end of February 2026.

agenda_briefs.title: Council to receive RCMP status report and annual policing plan update
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council will receive a status update on RCMP policing operations for December 2025 to January 2026, along with the Annual Performance Plan (APP) quarterly report for October to December 2025. This is a report for information only — no vote or bylaw is proposed at this time. The report covers recent policing activities and how the RCMP is performing against its annual plan. Business owners should be aware that this is a routine update, not a new policy or regulation.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
9.8
PSC - SU: RCMP STATUS REPORT (DECEMBER 2025 - JANUARY 2026) AND ANNUAL PERFORMANCE PLAN QUARTERLY REPORT (OCTOBER - DECEMBER 2025)
Purpose: To provide Council with a status update (SU) on policing operations covering December 2025 to January 2026 and the Annual Performance Plan (APP) quarterly report covering October to December 2025.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby Zoning Bylaw 2026 to replace 1965 version
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on a major update to the city's zoning rules. A proposed bylaw, called Burnaby Zoning Bylaw 2026, would repeal the current 1965 Zoning Bylaw and replace it with a completely new set of rules. This includes new Development Permit Area Guidelines (rules for getting permits to build or change a property) and a reassignment of zoning districts (which decides what you can do on your land, like run a business or build housing). This is a first, second, and third reading — meaning council is considering it for approval, but it is not law yet. A public hearing will likely be held before a final vote.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the public hearing date — this is your chance to speak up about how the new rules could affect your property or business.
agenda_briefs.industries: Construction & Development, Landlord & Property Mgmt, Retail
agenda_briefs.source_body:
11.1.1
#14815 - Burnaby Zoning Bylaw 2026
Purpose: to repeal Burnaby Zoning Bylaw 1965 and replace it with the proposed Burnaby Zoning Bylaw 2026, including new Development Permit Area Guidelines and zoning district reassignment

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby to update penalty rules for Zoning Bylaw 1965 violations
agenda_briefs.summary: Council will consider a proposed bylaw that would update the Burnaby Bylaw Notice Enforcement Bylaw. Specifically, it would remove the old penalty schedule for the Burnaby Zoning Bylaw 1965 and replace it with a new schedule for the updated Burnaby Zoning Bylaw 2026. This means if you own property or run a business in Burnaby, the fines and enforcement process for zoning violations (like illegal building use or sign rules) could change. The decision has not been made yet — council is set to vote on this amendment.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision — if passed, review the new penalty schedule for the Burnaby Zoning Bylaw 2026 to ensure your property or business is compliant.
agenda_briefs.industries: Construction & Development, Landlord & Property Mgmt, Retail
agenda_briefs.source_body:
11.1.2
#14816 - Burnaby Bylaw Notice Enforcement Bylaw 2009, Amendment Bylaw No. 4, 2026
Purpose: to amend the Burnaby Bylaw Notice Enforcement Bylaw 2009 (repeal Schedule A, Burnaby Zoning Bylaw 1965 section and replace with Burnaby Zoning Bylaw 2026)

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby to amend definitions of 'affordable housing' in fee waiver bylaw
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposed bylaw that would change the definitions of 'affordable housing' and 'Zoning Bylaw' in the current Waivers and Reductions of Amenity Cost Charges (ACC) and Development Cost Charges (DCC) Bylaw. Amenity Cost Charges and Development Cost Charges are fees the city charges developers to help pay for community services and infrastructure like parks, roads, and water pipes. If approved, the new definitions could affect which housing projects qualify for fee waivers or reductions, potentially impacting the cost and feasibility of future development projects in Burnaby.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision — this could affect which housing projects qualify for fee breaks in Burnaby.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
11.1.3
#14817 - Burnaby Waivers and Reductions of Amenity Cost Charges and Development Cost Charges Bylaw 2024, Amendment Bylaw No. 1, 2026
Purpose: to amend Burnaby Waivers and Reductions of ACC and DCC Bylaw 2024 (definitions of "affordable housing" and "Zoning Bylaw")

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby Tenant Protection Bylaw 2026 for redevelopment impacts
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on a proposed Tenant Protection Bylaw 2026. This bylaw would create new rules to protect tenants who are forced to move because their building is being redeveloped (torn down or renovated). The goal is to give tenants more rights and support during the redevelopment process. Council will consider this proposal at an upcoming meeting — it has not been approved yet.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision and public hearing dates — this bylaw could affect your property if you plan to redevelop.
agenda_briefs.industries: Landlord & Property Mgmt, Tenant, Construction & Development
agenda_briefs.source_body:
11.1.4
#14821 - Burnaby Tenant Protection Bylaw 2026
Purpose: to establish protections for tenants impacted by redevelopment

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby bylaw amendment to strengthen tenant protection enforcement
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposed amendment to the Burnaby Bylaw Notice Enforcement Bylaw. This change would update how the city enforces tenant protection rules. If approved, it could mean new or stronger penalties for landlords who break tenant protection bylaws. The goal is to give the city better tools to hold landlords accountable and protect renters. The decision has not been made yet — council will discuss and vote on this proposal at an upcoming meeting.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council meeting date — this could affect how tenant complaints against you are enforced.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
11.1.5
#14822 - Burnaby Bylaw Notice Enforcement Bylaw 2009, Amendment Bylaw No. 6, 2026
Purpose: to amend the Burnaby Bylaw Notice Enforcement Bylaw 2009 (tenant protection)

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby to vote on mid-year fee and charge updates for 2026
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposed bylaw that would update the City of Burnaby's Consolidated Fees and Charges Bylaw with mid-year changes for 2026. If approved, this would adjust various fees the city charges for permits, licences, services, and other municipal costs. The exact fee changes are not detailed in this agenda item, but the decision could affect how much you pay for business licences, development permits, or other city services. The vote is scheduled for an upcoming council meeting.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision — fee changes could affect your business licence or permit costs.

key_dates.label: Burnaby fee bylaw amendment vote — council meeting

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby council to finalize e-bike and scooter pilot rules
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on final adoption of a bylaw that would amend the existing Street and Traffic Bylaw to allow a pilot program for e-bikes and electric kick scooters. If approved, this would create new rules for where and how these devices can be used on city streets and paths. The proposed bylaw is up for final approval at an upcoming council meeting — it has not yet been passed, so the rules are not in effect yet.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision — if approved, new rules for e-bikes and scooters could affect your business operations or deliveries.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
11.2.1
#14811 - Burnaby Street and Traffic Bylaw 1961, Amendment Bylaw No. 1, 2026
Purpose: to amend Burnaby Street and Traffic Bylaw 1964 (e-bike and electric kick scooter pilot program)

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby e-bike and scooter pilot in parks
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on final adoption of a bylaw that would allow e-bikes and electric kick scooters in certain Burnaby parks as part of a pilot program. The proposed amendment to the Parks Regulation Bylaw would create new rules for where and how these devices can be used. If approved, the pilot would let riders use e-bikes and scooters on designated park paths and trails, but not on all park areas. The decision has not been made yet — this is a proposal up for approval at the next council meeting.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision — if approved, check which park trails are open to e-bikes and scooters before riding.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
11.2.2
#14812 - Burnaby Parks Regulation Bylaw 1979, Amendment Bylaw No. 1, 2026
Purpose: to amend the Burnaby Parks Regulation Bylaw 1979 (e-bike and electric kick scooter pilot program)

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby bylaw amendment to enforce e-bike and scooter pilot
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposed bylaw that would update the Burnaby Bylaw Notice Enforcement Bylaw to support a new pilot program for e-bikes and electric kick scooters. If approved, this change would allow the city to issue tickets and enforce rules for these vehicles during the pilot. The amendment is up for final adoption at an upcoming council meeting.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision — this could affect how e-bikes and scooters are regulated in your business area.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby Rates Bylaw 2026 sets new property tax rates
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on final adoption of the Burnaby Rates Bylaw 2026. This proposed bylaw would set the tax rates for all taxable land and buildings in the city for the 2026 tax year. It is based on sections 197 and 216 of the Community Charter, which give the city the authority to set property tax rates. If approved, this bylaw will determine how much property tax you will owe on your business property next year.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision on this bylaw — it will directly affect your 2026 property tax bill.

Community Charter

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby council to vote on abandoning rezoning for 3900 Grand Promenade
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposal to abandon a rezoning bylaw for 3900 Grand Promenade, 9850 Austin Road, and 9858/9898 Gatineau Place. The original rezoning, approved in September 2024, allowed two residential towers (80 and 73 storeys) with a 13-storey retail and office podium. The applicant now wants to remove the commercial office space and add 292 more market strata residential units instead. Council will decide whether to scrap the old plan and let the applicant submit a new one.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision — if approved, a new rezoning application may follow that could affect nearby property values and development plans.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
11.3.1
#14737 - Burnaby Zoning Bylaw 1965, Amendment Bylaw No. 6, 2025 - REZ #24-23 (3900 Grand Promenade, 9850 Austin Road, and 9858 and 9898 Gatineau Place)
Purpose: to amend the previous rezoning of the subject lands pursuant to REZ #18-32 (Bylaw No. 14447), which received Final Adoption on September 23, 2024, and included two market strata residential buildings of 80 and 73 storeys, respectively, inclusive of a 13-storey retail and office podium. The applicant is proposing to remove commercial office use and replace it with additional market strata residential use, which includes the proposal of 292 additional market strata units
